schools.summary: Gill: by 'elder' is meant not an elder in office (for elders by office are afterwards spoken of, with particular rules) but an elder in age, here opposed not to a private member of a church but to young men in age; the apostle is giving rules for rebuking members of churches according to their age; Paul charging that Timothy not sharply rebuke an aged man but entreat him as a father, and the younger men as brethren — the tender, age-fitting manner of admonition (Paul directing the spirit in which Timothy is to deal with those he must correct: an aged man not to be harshly 'rebuked,' sharply reproved, but 'intreated,' gently exhorted and appealed to, 'as a father,' with the reverence due to age, and 'the younger men' treated 'as brethren,' with the affection of an equal, so the minister's correction is always tempered by love and respect suited to each).

themes: rebuke not an elder, but intreat him as a father
and the younger men as brethren
the age-fitting manner of admonition
correction tempered by love and respect

schools.summary: Gill: the elder women, when they offend in any point, are to be reasoned, argued, and pleaded with as children should with their mothers, considered as mothers in Israel, and treated with great tenderness and respect; Barnes: showing still the same respect for age and for the proprieties of life; Paul directing that elder women be treated as mothers and the younger as sisters, with all purity — the honour and chastity required toward women in the church (Paul completing the rule of age-fitting respect: 'the elder women' regarded 'as mothers,' with reverence and tenderness, and 'the younger as sisters,' with brotherly affection, and adding a special guard for the latter, 'with all purity,' that the minister's dealings with younger women be marked by perfect chastity and freedom from all taint or appearance of impropriety).

themes: the elder women as mothers
the younger as sisters, with all purity
age-fitting honour toward women
the minister's dealings marked by all purity

schools.summary: Gill: the honour to be given them is not a putting of them into the office of a deaconess in the church (which office some think is referred to, and obtained in some primitive churches), but rather, as the context shows, the supplying of their needs, maintaining and relieving the truly destitute widows of the church; Paul charging that widows who are widows indeed be honoured — the support and esteem due to the truly destitute widow (Paul opening his instruction concerning widows by commanding that those 'that are widows indeed,' genuinely such, left alone, destitute, and without family to support them, be 'honoured,' a word that here carries the sense of practical care and maintenance as well as respect, so the church is to esteem and provide for the widow who has none else to help her).

themes: honour widows
that are widows indeed
the support due to the truly destitute widow
esteem joined to practical maintenance

schools.summary: Gill: such widows who have children or nephews (grandchildren) are not widows indeed; they are not desolate or without persons to take care of them, and their children or nephews should provide and not suffer the church to be burdened with them; Paul directing that if a widow have children or grandchildren, these should learn to show piety at home and requite their parents, for this is good and acceptable before God — the family's first duty to maintain its own (Paul guarding the church's charity by laying the first burden of a widow's care on her own kin: if she has 'children or nephews,' descendants, they are to 'learn first to shew piety at home,' to practise true godliness in caring for their own household, and 'to requite their parents,' repaying the care once given them, a plain natural and Christian duty that is 'good and acceptable before God,' so family love must do its part before the church is asked).

themes: if any widow have children or nephews
let them learn first to shew piety at home
and to requite their parents
family love doing its part before the church

schools.summary: Gill: a real widow is one whom the Jews call 'a perfect one,' in opposition to one divorced or a brother's widow who has had the shoe plucked off; Barnes: 'desolate' means solitary, alone; Paul describing the widow indeed as one who, being desolate, trusts in God and continues in supplications and prayers night and day — the portrait of the godly, praying widow who casts herself on God (Paul drawing the character of the widow truly deserving the church's care: she is 'desolate,' left utterly alone without husband or family, and in that very destitution she 'trusteth in God,' making him her support and hope, and 'continueth in supplications and prayers night and day,' a woman of devotion who, having no earthly stay, gives herself to constant prayer, so her loneliness is filled with God).

themes: she that is a widow indeed, and desolate, trusteth in God
continueth in supplications and prayers night and day
the portrait of the godly, praying widow
her loneliness filled with God

schools.summary: Gill: she that lives voluptuously and deliciously, a wanton, loose, and licentious life, serving divers lusts and pleasures, is meant; Barnes: the Greek word (occurring elsewhere only in James 5:5) means to live luxuriously or in self-indulgence; Paul declaring that the widow who lives in pleasure is dead while she lives — the spiritual deadness of the self-indulgent widow (Paul setting against the praying widow her opposite: the woman who 'liveth in pleasure,' in luxury, self-indulgence, and sensual ease, the slave of her appetites, and pronouncing her 'dead while she liveth,' for though alive in body she is dead in soul, void of spiritual life and of any title to the church's honour, a life given to pleasure being a kind of living death, the very reverse of the widow indeed who trusts in God).

themes: but she that liveth in pleasure
is dead while she liveth
the spiritual deadness of the self-indulgent
a life of pleasure a kind of living death

schools.summary: Gill: 'these things' concern rebuking persons of a different age and sex, the maintenance of widows, particularly that children provide for their parents when helpless, and that widows trust in God and give themselves to prayer and supplication, and not live in sin; Barnes: to announce or declare them; Paul directing that Timothy give these things in charge, that they may be blameless — the enforcing of the rules so that all be without reproach (Paul bidding Timothy not merely to know these directions but to 'give them in charge,' to enjoin them solemnly and publicly upon the people, and that with a clear aim, 'that they may be blameless,' that the widows, the families, and all concerned might so order their conduct as to be free from reproach, the rules given not to burden but to keep the church and its members unblamable).

themes: and these things give in charge
that they may be blameless
the enforcing of the rules for blamelessness
the rules given to keep all without reproach

schools.summary: Chrysostom: many consider their own virtue sufficient for salvation, and that if they regulate their own life nothing further is wanting; but in this they greatly err, proved by the man who buried his one talent and brought it back not diminished but entire, just as delivered, yet was condemned — for it is not enough to keep oneself, one must profit others; Paul declaring that if any provide not for his own, especially his own house, he has denied the faith and is worse than an infidel — the solemn duty of providing for one's family (Paul pressing the family's duty with the sharpest warning: the man who 'provide not for his own,' his relations, and 'specially for those of his own house,' his nearest family, parents, widows, and dependents, has by that neglect 'denied the faith,' contradicting in deed the love the gospel requires, and 'is worse than an infidel,' since even unbelievers by natural affection provide for their own, so to fail here is to sink below the very heathen).

themes: if any provide not for his own
specially for those of his own house
he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel
failing to provide sinks below the heathen

schools.summary: Gill: 'the number' is of widows to be maintained by the church (some take it of those chosen as deaconesses, with the care of the poor widows committed to them, as the Arabic renders 'if a widow be chosen a deaconess,' but the former sense is best); such were not to be taken in under sixty years old; Paul directing that a widow not be taken into the number under sixty, having been the wife of one man — the conditions for placing a widow on the church's roll (Paul, distinguishing which widows are to be formally enrolled for the church's support and service, setting two conditions: she must be 'under threescore years old' no longer, that is at least sixty, past the age of remarriage and of settled character, and 'having been the wife of one man,' of approved fidelity in marriage, not given to a loose or many-married life, so only widows of mature age and proven chastity are received onto the roll).

themes: let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old
having been the wife of one man
the conditions for enrolling a widow
mature age and proven chastity required

schools.summary: Gill: she must be well reported of for good works, both by the members of the church and by them that are without; Barnes: of good character or reputation, bringing up children (her own or others, in a proper manner), lodging strangers (hospitable to them); Paul listing that the enrolled widow be well reported of for good works — bringing up children, lodging strangers, washing the saints' feet, relieving the afflicted, following every good work — the proven life of holy service that marks the worthy widow (Paul completing the qualifications with a portrait of practical godliness: the widow 'well reported of for good works,' and naming them, the careful 'bringing up of children,' the hospitable 'lodging of strangers,' the humble 'washing of the saints' feet,' lowly service to fellow believers, the merciful 'relieving of the afflicted,' and in sum a diligent following of 'every good work,' so her past life of love and service is the credential for the church's honour).

themes: well reported of for good works
if she have brought up children, if she have lodged strangers
if she have washed the saints' feet, if she have relieved the afflicted
a life of holy service the widow's credential

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, having discoursed much concerning widows and settled the age at which they were to be admitted ('let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old') and described their qualifications, proceeds to the younger, who are not to be taken in because of the risk that their unsubdued desires will draw them back to a worldly life; Paul directing that the younger widows be refused, for when they wax wanton against Christ they will marry — the reason for not enrolling younger widows, the danger of unsettled desire (Paul bidding Timothy 'refuse,' not enrol, the 'younger widows,' and giving the reason: when such, having pledged themselves to the church's service, 'have begun to wax wanton against Christ,' when fleshly desire revives and draws them from their devotion to him, 'they will marry,' wishing to return to married life and so breaking the engagement they had made, so it is unwise to bind the young by such a pledge their changeable hearts may not keep).

themes: but the younger widows refuse
for when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ
they will marry
the young not bound by a pledge they may not keep

schools.summary: Barnes: it is rather 'having condemnation,' or incurring guilt — this does not mean of necessity eternal damnation but the guilt of breaking a solemn engagement; Paul declaring that such have condemnation because they have cast off their first faith — the guilt of breaking the pledge first given (Paul naming what such younger widows incur by abandoning the service they had vowed: 'having damnation,' better 'condemnation,' that is blame and guilt, 'because they have cast off their first faith,' they have broken the first pledge or promise of devotion they had made to serve Christ and the church in their widowhood, so the fault lies in the breaking of a solemn engagement, a thing that justly brings reproach).

themes: having damnation
because they have cast off their first faith
the guilt of breaking the first pledge
blame for casting off the engagement of devotion

schools.summary: Gill: being at ease and without labour, living at the expense of the church, they learn to be idle and 'wander about from house to house,' having nothing else to do (what the Jews called 'the gadding widow'); Barnes: if supported by the church and without the settled principles that might be expected in those more aged and experienced, other evils are to be feared; Paul observing that such widows learn to be idle, wandering from house to house, and become tattlers and busybodies, speaking what they ought not — the further evils of an idle, gossiping life (Paul naming the additional dangers of enrolling unfit younger widows: maintained without labour, they 'learn to be idle,' fall into habits of idleness, 'wandering about from house to house,' gadding from home to home with nothing to do, and the idleness breeding worse, they become 'tattlers,' gossips, 'and busybodies,' meddlers in others' affairs, 'speaking things which they ought not,' so idleness opens the door to a train of mischiefs).

themes: they learn to be idle, wandering about from house to house
tattlers also and busybodies
speaking things which they ought not
idleness breeding a train of mischiefs

schools.summary: Gill: 'the younger women' is rather 'the younger widows' (so some copies read), for this is not the apostle's advice to young women in general (though it will suit them) but to the younger widows in particular of whom he is speaking; Barnes: 'I will therefore' means 'I give it as my opinion,' or counsel; Paul counselling that the younger widows marry, bear children, guide the house, and give the adversary no occasion to reproach — the wholesome remedy of marriage and household duty (Paul, instead of enrolling the younger widows for idle dependence, giving the better course: let them 'marry' again, 'bear children,' and 'guide the house,' manage their households well, filling their lives with the honest duties of wife and mother, and so 'give none occasion to the adversary,' whether Satan or the church's enemies, 'to speak reproachfully,' that their busy, ordered home-life might silence all slander).

themes: I will therefore that the younger women marry, bear children, guide the house
give none occasion to the adversary
to speak reproachfully
the remedy of marriage and household duty

schools.summary: Gill: Paul means some of those younger widows whom he had known and observed to depart from the faith they first professed, turn their backs on Christ, and give themselves up to carnal lusts and pleasures and an idle and impure life and conversation, walking according to the course of this world and the prince of it, by whom they were led captive; Paul declaring that some younger widows are already turned aside after Satan — the proof from sad experience of the danger warned against (Paul confirming his counsel by pointing to cases already seen: 'some are already turned aside after Satan,' certain younger widows known to him having departed from their profession, abandoned the path of Christ, and followed Satan into a worldly, fleshly life, so the danger he warns of is no mere supposition but a thing that had already happened, making his cautious rule the wiser).

themes: for some are already turned aside
after Satan
the danger proved by real cases
Paul's caution resting on sad experience

schools.summary: Gill: if any member of a church, whether brother or sister, has mothers or grandmothers or any near relations who are widows in mean circumstances and incapable of taking care of themselves, they should relieve them; Paul directing that any believing man or woman who has widows relieve them, that the church not be charged but may relieve the widows indeed — the relief of one's own widows so the church may help the truly destitute (Paul, closing the matter, again laying the burden of a widow's support on her believing kindred: 'if any man or woman that believeth have widows' among their relations, 'let them relieve them' privately, 'and let not the church be charged,' burdened, with them, to the end 'that it may relieve them that are widows indeed,' the truly destitute who have no one else, so family responsibility frees the church's limited resources for those genuinely in need).

themes: if any man or woman that believeth have widows, let them relieve them
and let not the church be charged
that it may relieve them that are widows indeed
family relieving its own, freeing the church

schools.summary: Gill: by 'elders' here are meant not elders in age (though such ought to be honoured and maintained when reduced) but elders in office, particularly described as good rulers and labourers in the word; such are to be counted worthy of double honour; Paul directing that elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honour, especially those who labour in the word and doctrine — the honour and maintenance due to faithful, labouring elders (Paul turning to the church's duty toward its officers: 'the elders that rule well,' those who govern the church faithfully and wisely, to be counted 'worthy of double honour,' a double esteem and, as the next verse shows, double in the sense of including generous maintenance as well as respect, 'especially they who labour in the word and doctrine,' above all those who toil hard in preaching and teaching, the labour of the word deserving the fullest honour).

themes: let the elders that rule well be counted worthy of double honour
especially they who labour in the word and doctrine
double honour of esteem and maintenance
the toil of preaching deserving most

schools.summary: Barnes: this is adduced as a reason why a church should show all due respect and care for its ministers — the reason being that, as God took care to make provision for the labouring ox, much more should due attention be paid to those who labour for the welfare of the church; 'thou shalt not muzzle the ox' (see its bearing explained on such cases); Paul citing the Scripture, that the ox treading out the corn is not to be muzzled and the labourer is worthy of his reward, to ground the elder's maintenance — the two scriptural warrants for the minister's support (Paul backing the 'double honour' with two proofs: the law's word 'Thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn,' which applied means the labourer should share in the fruit of his toil, and the saying 'The labourer is worthy of his reward,' a principle of natural justice and the Lord's own word; Paul here calling both an Old Testament law and a saying of Christ 'the Scripture,' joining them as of equal authority, so the minister's right to support rests on God's own word).

debates.question: What does Paul call 'the Scripture' here?

sides.position: Both an Old Testament law ('thou shalt not muzzle the ox,' Deuteronomy 25:4) and a saying of Christ ('the labourer is worthy of his reward,' Luke 10:7) — joining them as of equal divine authority

themes: thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn
the labourer is worthy of his reward
the two scriptural warrants for the minister's support
law and Christ's saying joined as Scripture

schools.summary: Barnes: the word 'elder' here is used in the sense it bears in the previous verse, as relating to office and not to age (as in 5:1) — the connection demands this interpretation; 'receive not an accusation' means he was not to regard such a charge as proved or act on it unless duly attested; Paul charging that against an elder no accusation be received except before two or three witnesses — the protection of the minister from unsupported charges (Paul, having required the elder's honour, guarding his good name from malice: 'against an elder,' a church officer, Timothy to 'receive not an accusation,' not entertain or act on a charge, 'but before two or three witnesses,' only when supported by the testimony of two or three according to the rule of God's law, so a faithful minister, peculiarly exposed to slander, is not condemned on the word of a single accuser).

themes: against an elder receive not an accusation
but before two or three witnesses
the protection of the minister from unsupported charges
not condemned on a single accuser's word

schools.summary: Gill: Paul adds this to the above rule to show he was far from screening wicked ministers or elders guilty of flagitious crimes and gross enormities; though these words may apply to all offenders who are members of churches, they seem chiefly to regard elders who sin, who continue to sin; Paul charging that those who sin be rebuked before all, that others also may fear — the public rebuke of persistent offenders as a deterrent (Paul balancing the safeguard of v.19 with a charge against partiality: an elder, or any member, duly proved to 'sin,' and especially one who persists in open, notorious sin, to be 'rebuke[d] before all,' openly reproved in the presence of the church, not spared because of his office, and that with a clear purpose, 'that others also may fear,' the public reproof a salutary warning deterring others from the like sin).

themes: them that sin rebuke before all
that others also may fear
the public rebuke of persistent offenders
a deterrent, not sparing even the elder

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having spoken of bishops and deacons, of men and women, of widows and elders and all others, and having shown how great the authority of a bishop, now Paul, speaking of judgment, adds 'I charge thee before God and the Lord Jesus Christ and the elect angels, that thou observe these things without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality'; Paul solemnly charging Timothy, before God, the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels, to observe these things without partiality — the most solemn adjuration to judge without favour or prejudice (Paul, on the weighty matter of judging elders and offenders, laying the most solemn charge: adjuring Timothy 'before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels,' in the awful presence of the whole heavenly court, 'that thou observe these things,' the rules just given, 'without preferring one before another,' without bias or favouritism, and 'doing nothing by partiality,' that in all his judgments Timothy hold the strictest impartiality, as one who acts under the eye of heaven).

themes: I charge thee before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels
that thou observe these things
without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality
judging under the eye of heaven

schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be understood of removing censures from offenders upon repentance (which in later times was done by imposition of hands, though no such custom obtained in the apostle's time), but of the laying on of hands in ordination, which is not to be done suddenly and hastily, without due trial and examination of the man's fitness; Paul charging that hands be laid suddenly on no man, that Timothy not partake of others' sins, but keep himself pure — the caution in ordaining and the call to personal purity (Paul warning against rashness in setting men into office: 'lay hands suddenly on no man,' ordain none hastily without proving his fitness, lest by ordaining an unworthy man Timothy become 'partaker of other men's sins,' sharing the guilt of the harm such a one does, and adding the call to personal holiness, 'keep thyself pure,' that Timothy guard his own life clean, both from such complicity and from all defilement).

themes: lay hands suddenly on no man
neither be partaker of other men's sins
keep thyself pure
the caution in ordaining and the call to purity

schools.summary: Gill: it was commendable in Timothy to keep under his body by abstemious living, as the apostle did, not pampering the flesh or encouraging its lusts, so preserving purity and chastity; yet it was proper he should take care of his health, that it not be impaired by too much severity, lest he be incapable of doing the work of the Lord; Paul advising Timothy to drink no longer only water but use a little wine for his stomach and his frequent infirmities — the practical, fatherly care for the minister's health (Paul, with tender concern, breaking in with a word about Timothy's body: his young helper, given to a strict, abstemious life and drinking only water, suffering from a weak stomach and 'often infirmities,' and so counselling him to 'use a little wine' as a medicine, moderately, 'for thy stomach's sake,' so that, while temperance is good, the minister must not by needless austerity ruin the health he needs for God's service).

themes: drink no longer water, but use a little wine
for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities
fatherly care for the minister's health
temperance good, but health not ruined by austerity

schools.summary: Gill: some men are such open and notorious sinners that there is no need of any inquiry about them, any examination, or any witnesses to their character to pass judgment — they even prevent and supersede any formal process, and with such Timothy could have no difficulty; Paul observing that some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment, while other men's sins follow after — the discernment that some sins are plain and others come to light later (Paul, returning after the personal word of v.23 to the caution about ordaining and judging men, giving a maxim of discernment: 'some men's sins are open beforehand,' so manifest that they go before to judgment, plain to all without inquiry, while 'some men they follow after,' other men's sins hidden for a time and coming to light only later, so Timothy, in proving men for office, must reckon with both, not being deceived because a man's faults are not yet seen).

themes: some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment
and some men they follow after
the discernment of open and hidden sins
Timothy reckoning with both in proving men

schools.summary: Gill: the characters of some men are so fair and bright, and it is so clear a point that they have received the grace of God in truth, have rich furniture for the ministry, are sound in the doctrine of faith, and are men of godly lives, that they prevent any formal inquiry — their good works are manifest beforehand; Paul observing that the good works of some are manifest beforehand, and those otherwise cannot be hid — the assurance that worth, like sin, will in the end be revealed (Paul completing the maxim on its brighter side: as with sins, so with 'good works,' 'the good works of some are manifest beforehand,' plainly seen, marking the fit man at once, and even those that 'are otherwise,' not so evident, the quiet goodness hidden for a while, 'cannot be hid,' they too coming to light in time, so Timothy may take heart, for genuine worth, though sometimes unseen at first, will surely be made known, as truly as sin will).

themes: likewise also the good works of some are manifest beforehand
and they that are otherwise cannot be hid
the certainty that worth will be revealed
genuine goodness, like sin, coming to light
